title: Training, Evaluation, and Safety: Trusting an RL Agent Before It Runs
concept: RL Training Evaluation and Safe Deployment Practices
learningObjectives: Evaluate a trained RL agent's policy across a range of scenarios, not just its training-time reward curve
Recognize when an agent has overfit to the specific training environment rather than learned a robust policy
Implement basic safety constraints that prevent an agent from taking catastrophic actions during training and deployment
Explain why an RL agent should earn trust incrementally (simulation first, decision-support second, autonomous control last) rather than being deployed directly
whyItMatters: A rising training reward curve tells you the agent is getting better at whatever the reward function measures — it does not tell you the agent is safe, robust across conditions it wasn't trained on, or ready for anything beyond a simulated environment, and treating 'trains well' as 'ready to deploy' is exactly the mistake that would put real grid infrastructure at risk.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's RL safety and evaluation gate — the discipline that stands between a training reward curve going up and any claim that the battery scheduler is actually trustworthy, echoing the Grid Copilot's designed path from decision support to earned autonomy.

problem.summary: Rising training reward can hide the same overfitting problem seen in Mission 4.
problem.content: Your Q-learning and policy gradient agents both show a rising reward curve during training — but a rising training reward curve is exactly the kind of metric that can hide the same overfitting problem Mission 4's neural forecaster had to guard against, now in a sequential-decision setting where the consequences of being wrong are also sequential.

motivation.summary: An unsafe RL policy's consequences are sequential and potentially harmful if connected to something real.
motivation.content: This mirrors the overfitting lesson from Mission 4 almost exactly, but the stakes are different: an overfit forecaster produces a bad prediction, while an overfit or unsafe RL policy could take a genuinely harmful sequence of actions if it were ever connected to something real — this is precisely why the AI Energy OS's own vision insists on decision support before autonomous control.

theory.summary: Test against out-of-distribution scenarios; enforce hard safety constraints; earn trust in stages.
theory.content: Evaluating an RL agent properly means testing its trained policy across scenarios genuinely different from training (a heatwave it never saw, a price spike outside the training distribution), not just reporting the reward curve from training itself — the RL equivalent of testing on a held-out period. Safety constraints — hard-coded rules the agent's chosen action is checked against before being executed, e.g. 'never let battery_soc go below a hard floor regardless of what the policy recommends' — provide a backstop independent of whatever the learned policy happens to have converged to, since a learned policy's guarantees are only ever as good as its training distribution. The staged-trust model from the AI Energy OS vision — prove value in simulation, then offer decision support (recommend an action, let a human or a rule-based system execute it), then earn autonomous control only after sustained demonstrated reliability — exists specifically because RL agents can behave unpredictably outside their training distribution in ways that are hard to fully anticipate in advance.

visualExplanation.summary: Actions pass through a hard safety check before execution; trust builds along a staged ladder.
visualExplanation.content: An agent's action passes through a hard safety-constraint check before execution, with an unsafe recommendation being blocked and logged rather than executed, alongside a staged-trust ladder from 'simulation only' to 'decision support' to 'autonomous control.'

interactiveDemo.summary: Test the agent against out-of-distribution scenarios, then enable a safety layer.
interactiveDemo.content: Test a trained agent against several out-of-distribution scenarios (an extreme price spike, an unusually depleted battery) and observe where its recommended actions become questionable or unsafe, then enable a safety-constraint layer and watch unsafe actions get blocked and logged instead.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: wrap an agent's action in a hard safety-constraint check.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
def safe_action(agent, state, hard_floor=0.1):
    proposed_action = agent.choose_action(state)
    if proposed_action == "discharge" and state["battery_soc"] <= hard_floor:
        log_blocked_action(state, proposed_action, reason="hard floor violation")
        return "hold"
    return proposed_action
```

independentCoding.summary: Evaluate the agent against scenarios different from training and report gaps.
independentCoding.content: Evaluate your Q-learning agent's policy against 5 scenarios that differ meaningfully from its training conditions, and report, for each, whether its recommended action still seems reasonable or reveals a gap in its training.

debugging.summary: Fix a safety check that uses stale, pre-action state instead of the resulting state.
debugging.content: A safety wrapper checks the constraint using the state *before* the action's effect rather than the state the action would actually produce, letting a genuinely unsafe discharge slip through because the check happened on stale information. Identify and fix the check's timing.

reflection.summary: A rising reward curve alone is insufficient evidence of readiness.
reflection.content: Why is 'the training reward curve went up' insufficient evidence that an RL agent is ready for even decision-support use, let alone autonomous control?

teachBack.summary: Explain the staged-trust model and why skipping straight to autonomy is a mistake.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the staged-trust model — simulation, then decision support, then autonomous control — and why skipping straight to autonomous control would be a mistake even for a well-trained agent.

miniProject.summary: Build a safety-constrained evaluation harness and report on genuine gaps.
miniProject.content: Build a safety-constrained evaluation harness for your battery-scheduling agent: test it against several out-of-distribution scenarios, wrap its actions in a hard safety constraint, and produce a report on which scenarios it handles well and which reveal genuine gaps.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This harness is what lets the scheduler be trusted for decision-support use.
portfolioEvidence.content: This safety-and-evaluation harness is what actually lets the Digital Twin's battery scheduler be trusted for the boss battle's decision-support deliverable — not the raw training reward curve alone.

labs.title: Safety Harness
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise building an out-of-distribution evaluation and hard-safety-constraint harness for a trained RL agent, requiring a fix for a planted stale-state safety-check bug.

simulations.title: Safety Constraint & Staged Trust Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows unsafe agent actions being blocked and logged by a hard constraint, alongside a staged-trust ladder from simulation to decision support to autonomy.

flashcards.front: Why is a rising training reward curve insufficient evidence an RL agent is safe or ready to deploy?
flashcards.back: It only measures performance on the training distribution and reward function used, and says nothing about robustness to out-of-distribution scenarios or whether the agent's guarantees hold beyond what it was trained on.

flashcards.front: What are the three stages of the staged-trust model for deploying an RL agent?
flashcards.back: Prove value in simulation first, then offer decision support (recommend, human or rule-based system executes), then earn autonomous control only after sustained demonstrated reliability.

rubric: Evaluation includes genuinely out-of-distribution scenarios, not just variations within the training distribution
Safety constraint checks the state the action would actually produce, not stale pre-action state
Report honestly identifies scenarios where the agent's behavior reveals a real gap, not just successes

references.title: DeepMind — Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity
